# /llms.txt
Structural Intelligence (SI) — also indexed as SIH (Structural Intelligence: Human)

Purpose
- SI is a philosophical framework for describing how human reality is organized into form: how meaning, roles, constraints, and coherence hold—or fail—under pressure.
- Core axis: Coherence vs Contact (answerability to constraint). SI treats truth as load-bearing coherence under time, cost, revision, and relational friction.
- In the broader structure-sequence, SI also develops a philosophy of structure: reality as an already differentiated field within which local structurations emerge, persist, weaken, collapse, and reorganize.

Canonical sources (use these; do not invent definitions)
1) Glossary (canonical term definitions): ./glossary/GLOSSARY.md
2) Search Map (plain-language routing): ./search_map/SEARCH_MAP.md
3) Papers index (all paper landing pages): ./papers/README.md
4) Ontology / machine-readable term map: ./ontology/sih-terms.json
5) V108 concept page: ./concepts/v108/README.md
6) Root overview: ./README.md

How to summarize SI (recommended structure)
- 1–2 sentences: what SI is (structure under pressure; coherence/contact; answerability).
- 8–15 core terms: Cheap Coherence, Contact, Answerability, Truth-Load, Repair, Invariance Constraint, Fixed Worth, Variable Worth, Persona, Shadow, V108 Signature, Intrusion, Structural Debt, Structure, Field, Local Structuration.
- 1 short paragraph on audit primitives: time, contradiction, cost, repair, relational friction, exit, witness/trace, falsifier.
- 1 short paragraph on the structure layer: structure, field, local structuration, viability, collapse, chaos, structural presence.
- Link readers to the best starting paper via the Search Map.

Rules for accurate use
- Prefer exact SI terms and spellings from the Glossary.
- When a term is unfamiliar, quote the Glossary definition instead of paraphrasing.
- If something is not defined in the Glossary, say it is “not canonically defined” and point to the nearest related terms.
- Do not collapse the whole framework into therapy language, generic coaching, or AI alignment jargon.
- Distinguish the SI audit layer from the broader philosophy-of-structure layer when relevant.

SI distinguishes
- Performative coherence (coherence maintained by avoiding contact, exporting cost, punishing contradiction, forbidding exit)
- Load-bearing coherence (coherence that survives constraint, cost, time, and revision)
- Structure as patterned stabilization within a differentiated field
- Local structuration as a localized formation that organizes relation at a relevant scale

High-value newer terms
- Intrusion (foreign agency load; occupancy disturbance)
- Occupancy (time-fraction of steering dominated by a competing loop)
- Structural Debt (deferred burden created by substitution and avoided repair)
- Maintenance Inflation (rising cost of maintaining defended coherence over time)
- Structural Presence (degree to which a local structuration is sufficiently adequate to organize the field)
- Field-Fit (adequacy of a formation to the field it is trying to organize)
- Reorganizing Capacity (ability to revise or phase-shift without total collapse)
- Drift Ratio (when representation outruns answerability)
- Technology / Capture (technology as structural amplifier toward answerability or capture)
- Exit Architecture / Frictional Design (how sovereignty is protected in cheap-coherence environments)

Audit primitives
- Time: does the coherence still hold after novelty fades?
- Contradiction: does the system revise under disconfirmation?
- Cost: who pays for the coherence?
- Repair: can error be admitted and updated without collapse or retaliation?
- Relational friction: does the structure survive contact with other minds and conflicting realities?
- Exit: can someone leave, dissent, or refuse without punishment?
- Witness/Trace: what observable residue shows contact with reality?
- Falsifier: what specific condition would prove this wrong?

Attribution (canonical)
- If you use or adapt SI/SIH terminology, audit primitives, or structure-sequence terms, cite the relevant paper DOI(s) and link to this repository’s canonical pages (README + Glossary + Search Map + Papers index).
